name: TESTICULAR CANCER
definition: evolution of mixed germ cell tumor → into mature teratoma after chemotherapy (in malignant neoplasm with more than 1 germ cell tumor component excluding seminoma "monodermal dermoid" benign teratoma with only ectodermal components / squamous metaplasia of surface LCCSCT = most common histologic variant in children + adolescents primitive gonadal stroma tumor (exceedingly rare) containing sex cord-stromal elements blood-testis barrier to chemotherapy) AZZOPARDI TUMOR = REGRESSED GERM CELL TUMOR widespread metastatic disease + spontaneous regression of testicular malignancy ← development of 2 separate primary tumors ← NO lymphatic / vascular connections subsequent development of second primary tumor

demographics.Peak age: 25-35 years; 71-90 years; infants < 3 years: yolk sac tumor + teratoma;
demographics.Location: mostly unilateral; contralateral tumor develops eventually in 8%
demographics.Histo: may be different from that of primary tumor indicating totipotential nature of germ

dominant_finding: tumors isointense to testicular parenchyma on T1WI + T2WI
distribution: mostly unilateral; contralateral tumor develops eventually in 8%
discriminating_features: tumor < 1.5 cm is hypovascular in 86%, > 1.6 cm hypervascular in 95% (DDx: orchitis
tumors isointense to testicular parenchyma on T1WI + T2WI

center of mass of low SI ← keratin debris ± central calcification

indistinct margins of pulmonary metastases ← hemorrhage
pulmonary metastases (most common site of recurrent disease)

homogeneously isointense on T1WI + hyperintense on T2WI

low-level enhancement less than normal testis
frequent extension outside testis into epididymis
highly echogenic focal lesion (= scarred tumor residue)
metastases to retroperitoneum, mediastinum, cervical / axillary / supraclavicular lymph

dense echogenic foci ← calcified scars = "burned-out" tumor

clinical.Prognosis: > 93% [85-90%] 5-year survival rate for stage I [stage II]; complete remission
clinical.Spread: most aggressive testicular tumor, visceral metastases
